note:
在最近针对乔治·弗洛伊德(George Floyd)、布雷娜·泰勒(Breonna Taylor)和其他美国黑人死亡的抗议活动中，全国各地的警察部队使用了被归类为“不那么致命”的武器来驱散人群和制服个人。但是，尽管有标签，这些工具仍然可以超越肉体和骨骼的固有限制。听起来无害的设备-橡皮子弹，催泪瓦斯-被设计成通过武力和化学来迅速改变人类的行为。它们被作为一种可以立即杀死人的力量的替代品出售。然而，这样的武器并不是无害的。
约翰·霍普金斯大学(Johns Hopkins University)社会学助理研究科学家、“徽章无国界”(Badges Without Borders)一书的作者斯图尔特·施雷德(Stuart Schrader)指出，首先，它们经常被用作采取更严厉措施的前奏。他说：“称(武器)‘非致命’或‘不致命’是完全用词不当的一个原因是，如果它被用来强迫人们进入手持警棍的警察或手持步枪的士兵的[攻击范围]。”“只有在最严格的技术解读中，你才能称之为‘不那么致命’或‘非致命’。”而且，除了与致命武器结合使用外，这些工具本身仍可能造成重伤和死亡。
这类装置大致分为三类：钝力武器，旨在通过物理冲击伤害人，以及化学制剂，旨在刺激或使人丧失行动能力。第三组包括专门的技术，从手持泰瑟枪的电击到武器化的声波。
警察通常携带手持式工具-如警棍和盾牌-可以用来伤害人。在激烈的抗议活动中，一种更华丽的物品长期以来一直被广泛使用：包裹在橡胶中的金属弹，委婉地称之为橡胶子弹。从它们最早的使用开始，这些子弹就有造成持久伤害的历史。虽然官方指定的子弹可以向地面发射，然后反弹到人的腿上，但橡胶包裹的炮弹经常直接击中头骨、胸部或眼睛。在2009年的一项研究中，韦恩州立大学(Wayne State University)的研究人员发现，橡皮子弹击中的力量可以超过3500牛顿-比职业拳击手击中头部侧面的力量的两倍还多-这足以导致身体头骨骨折。
军队和警察在许多情况下都使用了橡皮子弹，从20世纪70年代的北爱尔兰开始，本月包括华盛顿特区。但它们只是一系列炮弹中的一个例子，有时被称为“指挥棒子弹”，长期以来一直被用作标准子弹的替代品。1969年12月的FBI执法公报描述了英国政府在香港控制骚乱时使用的一种这样的物品。香港当时是英国的附属领土。子弹有一个柚木弹壳，上面有一个小铅插件，由压缩气体发射。现在的木制指挥棒子弹更宽，更像冰球形状。从西雅图到弗格森，警方都在使用它们。除了橡胶和木头，还在使用的其他杀伤力较低的投射物包括塑料子弹，它也造成了伤亡，以及装满铅弹的枪支推进背包，被称为“豆袋子弹”，它以大致相同的方式使人丧失行动能力。
在过去的几周里，像这样的钝力投射物导致头骨骨折，颌骨骨折，并将伤势严重的人送往医院。一些人表示，他们在参与或报道乔治·弗洛伊德(George Floyd)抗议活动时，眼睛被警棍击中，他们表示正在组建一个支持小组。眼科医生专业协会写了一封公开信，要求警方停止使用包括橡皮子弹在内的损害眼睛的技术。
被广泛称为催泪剂的化学武器被正式归类为“催泪剂”，因为它们会导致泪水-伴随着恶心、灼热感、流泪和呼吸困难的感觉。投掷的催泪瓦斯罐和手榴弹会产生外观类似烟雾的粉末云，像薄雾一样在空中翻滚。当与身体接触时，这种粉末会产生上述反应，使人丧失行为能力，因此他们不能自愿抵抗警察。
催泪瓦斯最古老的类型之一是化合物CS(2-氯苄基丙二腈)，它形成于1928年，在20世纪60年代得到普及，主要有两个用途：一是作为支持美军在越南采取致命行动的武器，二是作为一种非致命性的方式来控制美国的人群-其中许多人抗议警察的暴行和越南战争。施雷德在无国界徽章上写道：“像美军那样称CS[a]为‘暴乱控制剂’，是为了使其在越南的使用合法化，使其不再引起争议。”
20世纪50年代进行的军事研究将CS归类为非致命性疾病。但今天的专家表示，这些研究在设计和适用性方面存在局限性，应该持怀疑态度对待。“催泪瓦斯是用来驱散和刺激的。但它的设计和首次使用是在人们认为它将被用来对付健康的工作年龄男性的时代，“丹·卡泽塔(Dan Kaszeta)说，他研究对化学和生物武器的防护，在他30年的职业生涯中，有一些时间是在美国陆军化学军和特勤局度过的。实验室研究还没有揭示催泪瓦斯对其他人口统计数据或有既往疾病的人的影响。Kaszeta说：“没有办法构建一项伦理实验室研究来调查儿童、哮喘患者、COPD(慢性阻塞性肺疾病)患者、[结核病]患者或孕妇。”他补充说，女性对催泪瓦斯的反应也没有得到很好的研究。
在实验室之外，催泪瓦斯已经被证明会对哮喘患者造成长期的损害，并可能导致流产。当在狭小和通风不良的空间使用时，它也被认为与死亡有关。催泪瓦斯如果射向抗议者的头部，本身就可能是致命的，就像2019年10月和11月在伊拉克发生的那样。
其他最常见的用于平民的化学武器是胡椒喷雾。它们最初是作为一种动物驱避剂而设计的，源自辣椒植物对哺乳动物的天然防御。警方从20世纪80年代开始将这种喷雾剂用于人体。理论上，警方手册将喷雾概括为一种非常短距离的工具，用于驱散人群或防止对警察、旁观者或财产的直接伤害。但在实践中，警方也使用这些武器对付坐着的和手无寸铁的抗议者。它们会引起皮肤的灼热感，产生类似催泪瓦斯的效果，包括流泪和呼吸困难。
远程声学设备(LRAD)是作为警察扩音器和扩音器的替代品而销售的。虽然它当然能够大声传输信息，但它的设计初衷是先警告人们，然后使他们丧失行动能力：该设备产生的声波被刻意设计成能够携带远距离和高音量。它击中的目标高达152分贝-足以造成永久性听力损伤。LRAD大小不一，通常类似于一个大而平的扬声器。它们可以安装在三脚架上，但最常见的是安装在车辆上，包括专用卡车和直升机。
LRAD对抗议者的使用至少可以追溯到2009年匹兹堡G20抗议活动。2014年，纽约市警察局因使用LRAD对付抗议者而被起诉，美国第二巡回上诉法院(U.S.Court of Appeals for the Second Circuit)裁定，“根据明确确立的法律，故意使用LRAD的方式能够造成严重伤害，将非暴力抗议者转移到人行道上，违反了第十四修正案。”记者安德鲁·尼夫(Andrew Neef)在匹兹堡抗议活动中经历了LRAD，他在2018年的一篇文章中描述了这一影响。“你的大脑感觉就像在桌子上的一碗果冻里震动，”他告诉“亚利桑那镜报”。
闪光手榴弹，也被称为眩晕手榴弹，起源于1977年由英国特种部队支持的人质营救任务。闪光刘海既可以作为手榴弹类装置存在，也可以作为标准40毫米榴弹发射器中的弹丸存在。“闪光”是一种突如其来的强光，会导致眼睛疼痛、混乱和暂时性失明。“砰”声超过165分贝(比猎枪还响)。
这些工具的设计是为了让附近任何没有准备好接受声音或光线的人丧失行动能力，并为扔它们的警察或士兵的行动提供便利。除了作为人群控制措施外，警方还利用手榴弹在强行进入建筑物和住宅时获得突击元素。
据报道，一名上个月在西雅图遭遇闪光弹的抗议者被弹片打伤。总体而言，警察的闪光刘海造成了伤亡，包括烧伤和致命的心脏病发作。
泰瑟枪是一个著名的品牌，已经成为正式名称为“电子控制武器”的设备的统称。它们是一种手持工具，可以发射一对飞镖，目的是在35英尺外的目标将飞镖钩入人的皮肤。细铜线从省道延伸回设备，使持用者能够发出强大的电击。
虽然正式被描述为明显致命的子弹的一种不那么致命的替代品，但路透社(Reuters)2017年对700多名被这种武器震惊后死亡的人进行的调查发现，在这些死亡中，有五分之一是由这种武器造成的。亚特兰大的六名警察在5月30日的抗议活动中使用这些装置迫使两名学生下车，他们被控咄咄逼人使用武力。
note_en:
During the recent protests over the deaths of George Floyd, Breonna Taylor and other Black Americans, police forces across the country have used weapons categorized as          “less lethal” to disperse crowds and subdue individuals. But despite their label, these tools can still overstep the inherent limitations of flesh and bone. Devices that sound innocuous— rubber bullets, tear gas—are designed to quickly change human behavior through force and chemistry. And they are sold as an alternative to the kind of force that immediately kills. Such weapons are not harmless, however.
  For starters, they are often used as a prelude to more severe measures, notes Stuart Schrader, an assistant research scientist in sociology at Johns Hopkins University and author of the book  Badges without Borders.          “One of the reasons it’s a complete misnomer to call [a weapon]          ‘nonlethal         ’ or          ‘less lethal’ [is] if it         ’s being used to force people into the [attack range] of cops with batons—or soldiers with rifles,” he says.          “In only the most strict technical reading could you call that          ‘less lethal         ’ or          ‘nonlethal.’” And in addition to their use in combination with lethal weapons, these tools can still cause serious injury and death on their own.
  Such devices fall roughly into three categories: There are blunt-force weapons, which are designed to hurt people with a physical impact, and chemical agents, which are meant to irritate or incapacitate. A third group comprises specialized technologies ranging from the electric shocks of handheld Tasers to weaponized sound waves.
    Police commonly carry handheld implements—such as batons and shields—that can be employed to injure people. During intense protests, a flashier item has long seen widespread use: metal projectiles encased in rubber, euphemistically termed rubber bullets. From their earliest uses,  these bullets have a history of  causing lasting injury. While officially designated as rounds that can be  fired at the ground to then bounce into  people         ’s  legs, the rubber-encased projectiles often directly hit skulls, chests or eyes. In a 2009 study, researchers at Wayne State University found rubber bullets can hit with a force greater than 3,500 newtons—more than  twice as hard as being punched in the side of the head by a professional boxer—which was enough to  fracture cadaver skulls.
  Militaries and police have employed rubber bullets in many situations, beginning in  Northern Ireland in the 1970s and including  Washington, D.C., this month. But they are just one example of a family of projectiles, sometimes called          “baton rounds,” that have long been used as an alternative to standard bullets. The  December 1969 issue of the  FBI Law Enforcement Bulletin described one such item used by the British government for riot control in Hong Kong, then a dependent territory of the U.K. The bullet had a teak shell with a small lead insert and was fired by compressed gas. Present-day wooden baton rounds are broader and more puck-shaped. And they have been used by police from  Seattle to  Ferguson. Beyond rubber and wood, other less lethal projectiles still in use include plastic bullets, which have also  caused injuries and deaths, as well as gun-propelled satchels full of lead shot, called          “beanbag rounds,” which incapacitate people in much the same way.
  In the past couple of weeks, blunt-force projectiles like these have fractured skulls, broken jaws and sent people to the hospital in  critical condition. A number of people who say they have lost eyes to baton rounds while participating in or covering the George Floyd protests have indicated they are  forming a support group. And a professional association of ophthalmologists has written an open letter asking police to stop using eye-damaging techniques, including rubber bullets.
    The chemical weapons broadly termed tear gas are officially classified as          “lachrymatory agents” because they result in tears—along with nausea, burning sensations, watering eyes and a feeling of breathing difficulty. Thrown canisters and grenades of tear gas produce clouds of powder with an appearance akin to smoke that billow in the air like a mist. On contact with the body, the powder produces the aforementioned reactions, which incapacitate people so they cannot voluntarily resist police.
  One of the oldest types of tear gas is the compound CS (2-chlorobenzalmalononitrile), which was formulated in 1928 and popularized in the 1960s for two main uses: as a weapon in support of lethal action by the U.S. military in Vietnam and as a nonlethal way to control crowds—many of them protesting police brutality and the Vietnam War—in the U.S.          “To call CS [a]          ‘riot control agent,’ as the US military did,” Schrader wrote in  Badges without Borders,          “was to legitimize its use in Vietnam and make it uncontroversial,” despite the fact that          “CS was not used in riot control as much as in combat.”
  Military studies performed in the 1950s classified CS as nonlethal. But experts today say these studies had limitations in design and applicability and should be treated with some skepticism.          “Tear gas is designed to disperse and irritate. But it was designed and first used in an era when it was assumed it would be used against healthy, working-age males,” says Dan Kaszeta, who studies protection against chemical and biological weapons and has spent some of his 30-year career in the U.S. Army’s Chemical Corps and the Secret Service. Lab studies have not revealed the effects tear gas can have on other demographics or people with preexisting conditions.          “There         ’s no way to structure an ethical lab study to investigate children, asthmatics, COPD [chronic obstructive pulmonary disease] sufferers, [tuberculosis] patients or pregnant women,” Kaszeta says. He adds that  women         ’s reactions to tear gas are also not well-studied.
  Outside of the lab, tear gas has been shown to cause prolonged damage in  people with asthma and to potentially contribute to miscarriages. It has also been linked to death when used in confined and poorly ventilated spaces. Tear gas canisters themselves can be lethal if fired at  protesters’ head, as they were in Iraq in October and November 2019.
  The other most common kinds of chemical weapons used on civilians are  pepper sprays. They were originally devised as an animal repellent, derived from chili plants’ natural defense against mammals. Police adopted the sprays for use on humans  starting in the 1980s. In theory, police manuals outline the sprays as a very short-range tool to disperse crowds or prevent immediate harm to officers, bystanders or property. But in practice, police have also used these weapons against  sitting and unarmed protesters. They can cause a burning sensation on the skin and produce effects similar to tear gas, including watering eyes and breathing difficulty.
    The long-range acoustic device (LRAD) is marketed as an alternative to police bullhorns and loudspeakers. While it is certainly capable of transmitting messages loudly, it was designed to first warn people and then incapacitate them: The device produces soundwaves deliberately shaped to carry far and at a high volume. It hits a target at up to 152 decibels—enough to cause  permanent hearing damage. LRADs come in a range of sizes, most often resembling a large, flat speaker. They can be mounted on a tripod but are most commonly incorporated in vehicles, including dedicated trucks and helicopters.
  LRAD use against protesters dates at least as early as the G20 protests in Pittsburgh in 2009. In 2014 the New York City Police Department was sued for turning LRADs against protesters, and the U.S. Court of Appeals for the Second Circuit held that          “   purposefully using a LRAD in a manner capable of causing serious injury to move non-violent protesters to the sidewalks violates the Fourteenth Amendment under clearly established law.” Journalist Andrew Neef, who  experienced an  LRAD in the Pittsburgh protests, described the effect in a 2018 article.          “Your brain feels like it         ’s vibrating in a bowl of jelly on the table,” he told the Arizona Mirror.
    Flash bang grenades, also known as stun grenades, have their origins in a  hostage rescue mission supported by British special forces in 1977. Flash bangs exist as both hand-thrown grenadelike devices and projectiles that fit into standard 40-millimeter grenade launchers. The          “flash” is a sudden bright light that causes ocular pain, confusion and  temporary blindness. The          “bang” is more than 165 decibels (louder than a shotgun).
  These tools are designed to incapacitate anyone nearby who is not prepared for the sound or light and to facilitate the movement of the police or soldiers who toss them. In addition to their use as crowd-control measures, police have also utilized the grenades to gain the element of surprise when entering buildings and  homes by force.
  A protestor encountering a flash bang in Seattle last month was  reported to have been injured by shrapnel. And in general, police flash bangs have caused injuries and deaths, including  burns and fatal  heart attacks.
    Taser is a well-known brand name that has become the catch-all term for devices formally known as          “electronic control weapons.”         They are handheld tools that launch a pair of darts designed to hook into a person         ’s skin at a target  up to 35 feet away. Thin copper wires extend from the darts back to the device, allowing the wielder to deliver a powerful electric shock.
  While formally described as a less lethal alternative to explicitly lethal bullets, a  2017 Reuters investigation of more than 700 people who died after being shocked with the weapon found it to be a contributing factor in one fifth of those deaths. Six police officers in Atlanta who used these devices on a pair of students to force them out of their car during  protests on May 30 have been charged for an aggressive use of force.
title: “不那么致命”的武器有什么作用？

note:
根据古代DNA分析，一名埋葬在纽格兰奇通道坟墓内室的男子是兄弟姐妹结合的产物。他与埋葬在150公里外通道墓穴中的人的亲戚关系也更远，这表明，在新石器时代的农民首次从欧洲大陆抵达爱尔兰后的几个世纪里，相关统治者的网络控制了爱尔兰。这一发现让我们对石器时代的最后阶段和农业的开始以及该地区定居的村庄生活有了新的认识。
公元11世纪，爱尔兰米斯郡的一个人终于写出了一个流传了大约4000年的淫秽民间故事。根据这个故事，一位来自一个神族的古代国王在冬至与他的妹妹睡觉，这是一个魔法仪式的一部分，目的是重新启动太阳的日常周期，将世界从无尽的黑夜中拯救出来。据推测，这对夫妇是在该县的一个巨大的墓丘上做的这件事，当地人称之为Fertae Chuile，或称罪恶山。
今天，我们知道这座山是唐斯通道墓，这是一种通过一个用大石头标记的入口到达埋藏部分的建筑。道斯是更著名的纽格兰奇通道墓的近邻。每年冬至，太阳都会透过纽格兰奇的石头通道照亮最里面的墓室。在当地传说首次被记录下来近一千年后，古代DNA表明，故事的至少一部分-自然是最令人不安的部分-实际上是真实的。
这项研究的合著者之一、都柏林圣三一学院遗传学家罗斯·毛劳因(RosÓMaoldúin)说：“这个神话中神奇的太阳操纵已经让学者们质疑口头传统能持续多久。”“现在发现乱伦的潜在史前先例是非同寻常的。”
在圣三一遗传学家Lara Cassidy的带领下，Maoldúin和她的同事从一名男子的脱节骨骼中进行了DNA测序，该男子被安葬在纽格兰奇墓穴通道尽头的房间里。这名男子约四分之一的基因组由非常长的纯合子DNA组成：两个基因拷贝完全相同的DNA，而不是大多数人从父母那里继承的混合配对。这有力地表明，他的父母是一级亲属-很可能是兄妹。
这指向了同样来自都柏林三一学院的研究合著者丹尼尔·G·布拉德利(Daniel G.Bradley)所说的“一种极端的等级制度，以至于唯一配得上精英的伴侣就是家庭成员。”普通的君主通常不会嫁给他们的兄弟姐妹；即使是19世纪末的欧洲统治者也不得不满足于第一表亲或第二表亲来维持他们的王室血统。在国王实际上被认为是神的社会里，统治者只倾向于与他们的兄弟姐妹结婚，而神的“神性使他们免受社会习俗的影响”。
这是古埃及的法老、殖民前夏威夷的国王和印加皇帝的共同之处-显然，他们与5000年前爱尔兰不朽的陵墓建造统治者也有共同之处。冬至神话可能帮助这些统治者支持了他们声称自己是神的说法，并团结了移动20万吨岩石建造纽格兰奇这样的通道坟墓所需的不朽(不是遗憾)努力。
苏格兰国家博物馆的考古学家艾莉森·谢里登(Alison Sheridan)在一篇评论基因研究的文章中写道：“对早期的农民来说，标记冬至是至关重要的，他们需要知道白天什么时候会开始变长。”“也许这些精英声称通过‘控制’太阳的运动而拥有神圣的力量。”
而埋葬在纽格兰奇通道墓穴中的神王显然是这类统治者中人脉深厚的一部分，他们统治整个爱尔兰岛至少有一千年之久。卡西迪和她的同事对岛上其他43个新石器时代遗址的基因组进行了测序。事实证明，纽格兰奇人与埋葬在另外至少三座大型通道坟墓中的人有亲缘关系，其中两座位于纽格兰奇以西150公里(93英里)处。
根据他们的基因组，埋葬在纽格兰奇的男子是埋葬在斯莱戈县的Carrowmore和Carrowkeel Passage坟墓以及爱尔兰东北海岸的米林湾巨石墓穴的人的六级亲属。六级亲属可以是二表亲、两次离异的表亲、曾曾孙/父母、曾叔伯或姑姑(或侄女或侄子)。就像史前版本的哈布斯堡家族一样，这一庞大的亲属群体似乎统治着爱尔兰，并维持了几个世纪以来与之相关的王朝。
这些爱尔兰古代统治家族的基因线索为建造散布在爱尔兰、英国、西班牙和法国大西洋海岸的巨石坟墓的人们的政治和社会结构提供了许多线索。
生活在这个复杂的、等级分明的农业社会中的人们似乎在公元前3800年左右通过海路到达了爱尔兰。根据卡西迪和她的同事们的说法，新石器时代早期生活在法国和西班牙的人最有可能是稍晚的新石器时代爱尔兰人的来源，他们对他们的基因组进行了测序-以及英国的其他新石器时代人。那些航海的农民可能是从法国和西班牙航行到英国殖民的，后来又从英国航行到爱尔兰。
一旦这些早期的农民到达爱尔兰，他们很快就压倒了在那里生活了4000年的狩猎采集者。古老的DNA表明，新来的人吸收了爱尔兰的土著人口，而不是消灭他们；一名埋葬在公元前3700至3500年间帕卡宾纳宫廷坟墓的人在他最近的祖先中有一些原始狩猎-采集者的痕迹。但来自英国的新来者不仅带来了他们的DNA，他们还带来了农业、新工具和巨型建筑，比如纽格兰奇和卡洛莫尔的通道坟墓。
但是，尽管这种情况看起来最有可能，但我们需要更多的数据才能确定细节。谢里登写道：“许多基因拼图仍然缺失，需要对新石器时代的法国人进行更多的分析，以解决爱尔兰第一批农民的起源问题。”
note_en:
A man buried in the inner chamber of Newgrange Passage tomb was the product of a union between siblings, according to ancient DNA analysis. He was also more distantly related to people buried at passage tombs up to 150km away, suggesting that a network of related rulers controlled Ireland in the centuries after populations of Neolithic farmers first reached the island from mainland Europe. The find gives us new insight into the last phase of the Stone Age and the beginning of agriculture and settled village life in the area.
  In the 11 th century CE, someone in County Meath, Ireland, finally wrote out a salacious folktale that had been passed down for about 4,000 years. According to the story, an ancient king, who hailed from a tribe of gods, had slept with his sister on the winter solstice as part of a magic ritual to restart the Sun’s daily cycle and save the world from endless night. The couple supposedly did the deed in one of the county’s huge burial mounds, which the locals named Fertae Chuile, or the Hill of Sin.
  Today, we know that hill as the Dowth Passage tomb, a construction in which buried sections are reached through an entry marked by large stones. Dowth is a close neighbor of the more famous Newgrange Passage tomb. And every winter solstice, the Sun shines through the stone passage at Newgrange and lights up the innermost burial chamber. And nearly a thousand years after the local legend was first written down, ancient DNA suggests that at least part of the story—the most troubling part, naturally—was actually true.
 “The magical solar manipulations in this myth already had scholars questioning how long an oral tradition could survive,” said Trinity College Dublin geneticist Ros Ó Maoldúin, a coauthor on the study. “To now discover a potential prehistoric precedent for the incestuous aspect is extraordinary.”
 Maoldúin and her colleagues, led by Trinity geneticist Lara Cassidy, sequenced DNA from the disarticulated bones of a man interred in the chamber at the end of the Newgrange tomb’s passage. About a quarter of the man’s genome consisted of very long stretches of homozygous DNA: DNA where both copies of the genes were the same, instead of the mix-and-match pairs most people inherit from their parents. That strongly suggests that his parents were first-degree relatives—probably brother and sister.
  That points to what study coauthor Daniel G. Bradley, also of Trinity College Dublin, described as “a hierarchy so extreme that the only partners worthy of the elite were family members.” Ordinary monarchs don’t usually marry their siblings; even the rulers of late 19 th century Europe had to settle for first or second cousins to maintain their royal bloodlines. Rulers only tend to marry their siblings in societies where the king is actually considered a god, whose “perceived divinity exempts them from social custom.”
 That’s something the pharaohs of ancient Egypt, the kings of pre-colonial Hawai’i, and the Inca emperors had in common—and apparently something they shared with the monumental tomb-building rulers of Ireland 5,000 years ago. The winter solstice myth probably helped these rulers support their claims of being gods and rally the monumental (not sorry) effort it took to move 200,000 tons of rock to build passage tombs like Newgrange.
 “Marking the winter solstice was crucial for early farmers, who needed to know when the days would start to get longer,” wrote archaeologist Alison Sheridan, of National Museums Scotland, in an essay commenting on the genetic study. “Perhaps this elite claimed to have divine power by ‘controlling’ the Sun’s movement.”
   And the god-king buried in the Newgrange passage tomb was apparently part of a well-connected class of such rulers who held sway over the entire island of Ireland for at least a thousand years. Cassidy and her colleagues sequenced 43 other genomes from Neolithic sites around the island. It turned out that the Newgrange man was related to people buried in at least three other large passage tombs, including two located 150km (93 miles) west of Newgrange.
 According to their genomes, the man buried in Newgrange was a sixth-degree relative of people buried at Carrowmore and Carrowkeel Passage tombs in County Sligo and the Millin Bay Megalith on Ireland’s northeast coast. Sixth-degree relatives could be second cousins, first cousins twice removed, great-great-great-great grandchildren/parents, or great-great-great uncles or aunts (or nieces or nephews). Like a prehistoric version of the Habsburgs, this extended group of relatives seems to have ruled Ireland and maintained its connected dynasties for centuries.
 These genetic clues to Ireland’s ancient ruling families shed a lot of light on the political and social structure of the people who built the megalithic tombs that dot the Atlantic coasts of Ireland, Britain, Spain, and France.
   The people who lived in this complex, hierarchical farming society seem to have reached Ireland by sea sometime around 3,800 BCE. According to Cassidy and her colleagues, people living in France and Spain during the early part of the Neolithic period were the mostly likely source for the slightly later Neolithic Irish people whose genomes they sequenced—along with other Neolithic people in Britain. Those seafaring farmers probably sailed from France and Spain to colonize Britain and later sailed from Britain to Ireland.
 Once those early farmers reached Ireland, they quickly overwhelmed the hunter-gatherers who had been living there for the previous 4,000 years. Ancient DNA suggests that the newcomers absorbed Ireland’s indigenous population rather than wiping them out; a man buried at the Parkabinna court tomb between 3,700 and 3,500 BCE had some traces of those original hunter-gatherers among his recent ancestors. But the newcomers from Britain didn’t just bring their DNA; they brought farming, new tools, and megalithic architecture like the passage tombs at Newgrange and Carrowmore.
 But although that scenario looks like the most plausible, we need more data before we can be sure of the details. “Many pieces of the genetic jigsaw are still missing, and more analyses of Neolithic French individuals will be needed to settle the question of the origins of Ireland’s first farmers,” wrote Sheridan.
title: 乱伦的国王可能建造了爱尔兰的纽格兰奇通道坟墓
title_en: Incestuous kings may have built Ireland’s Newgrange passage tomb

note_en:
I guess this time it will be a fair performance comparison as both languages are compiled and statically typed.
     I’m curious to find out how Go and Crystal perform in comparison to each other.
  For the tests we will be running previously compiled programs. We will use the release flag to enable optimizations in Crystal:
  Go binaries don’t have a release version and we won’t be using any flags. So, it’s just:
   Alright, first we will write code to generate a Fibonacci sequence for a given number. Let’s find the 47th number which is 2,971,215,073.
  package  main import  "fmt" func  fibonacci ( n  uint32 )  uint32  {  if  n  <  2  {  return  n  }  return  fibonacci ( n - 1 )  +  fibonacci ( n - 2 ) } func  main ()  {  fmt . Println ( fibonacci ( 47 )) }
  def  fibonacci ( n  :  UInt32 )  return  n  if  n  <  2  fibonacci ( n - 1 )  +  fibonacci ( n - 2 )  end puts  fibonacci ( 47 )
     Crystal’s binary size is 5 times smaller than Go’s. Though, they can be slightly reduced in size when we omit the debug information:
   Also, not in this particular example, but generally Go’s compilation time is much much faster than Crystal’s.
  Now, let’s create a simple HTTP server using standard libraries. Both Go’s  net/http and Crystal’s  http/server employ concurrency: Go uses goroutines and Crystal uses fibers.
  package  main import  (	 "fmt"	 "net/http" ) func  main ()  {	 http . HandleFunc ( "/" ,  HelloServer )	 http . ListenAndServe ( ":8080" ,  nil ) } func  HelloServer ( w  http . ResponseWriter ,  r  * http . Request )  {	 fmt . Fprintf ( w ,  "Hello from %s!" ,  r . URL . Path [ 1 : ]) }
  require  "http/server" server  =  HTTP :: Server . new  do  | context |  context . response . content_type  =  "text/plain"  context . response . print  "Hello from  #{ context . request . path } !" end puts  "Listening on http://127.0.0.1:8080" server . listen ( 8080 )
 For benchmarking we will be using  wrk. If you’re not familiar with this tool it’s like a pretty well known ApacheBench (ab) but a modern version.
